text: A 3 m long steel wire is stretched to increase its length by 0.3 cm. Poisson's ratio for steel is 0.26. The lateral strain produced in the wire is
explanation: Longitudinal strain = \(\frac{\Delta l}{l} = \frac{0.3 \times 10^{-2}}{3} = 10^{-3}\). Poisson's ratio \(\sigma = \frac{\text{lateral strain}}{\text{longitudinal strain}} = 0.26\). Therefore, lateral strain = \(0.26 \times 10^{-3}\).
name: Questions Item 1

text: When a weight of 10 kg is suspended from a copper wire of length 3 m and diameter 0.4 mm, its length increases by 2.4 cm. If the diameter of the wire is doubled, then the extension in its length will be
explanation: Young's modulus \(Y = \frac{mg l}{\pi r^2 \Delta l}\). For first case, \(Y = 10^{11}\) Nm^{-2}. When diameter is doubled, new radius is twice, so area becomes 4 times. New \(\Delta l = \frac{400 \times 3}{\pi (4 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times 10^{11}} = 0.6\) cm.
name: Questions Item 2

text: The diagram shows stress versus strain curve for the materials A and B. From the curves, we can infer that
options: A is brittle but B is ductile
A is ductile but B is brittle
Both A and B are ductile
Both A and B are brittle
explanation: Material A has a yield point followed by plastic deformation, indicating ductility. Material B shows linear behaviour till fracture without yielding, indicating brittleness.
name: Questions Item 3

text: A force F is needed to break a copper wire having radius R. The force needed to break a copper wire of radius 2R will be
explanation: Breaking force \(F \propto\) cross-sectional area \(A = \pi r^2\). For radius 2R, new area is 4 times. Hence, new force = 4F.
name: Questions Item 4

text: If a pressure of \(8 \times 10^8\) N m^{-2} is applied to a lead block, so that its volume reduces by 20%. The Bulk modulus of lead block is
explanation: Bulk modulus \(B = \frac{p}{\Delta V / V}\). \(\Delta V / V = 0.20\). Therefore, \(B = \frac{8 \times 10^8}{0.20} = 4 \times 10^9\) Nm^{-2}.
name: Questions Item 5

text: What should be the diameter of a copper wire (Y = 12 × 10^{10} Nm^{-2}) of length 5 m to produce the same elongation produced by a 5 m long aluminium wire (Y = 7 × 10^{10} Nm^{-2}) of diameter 3 mm with the same 40 kg mass?
explanation: Elongation \(\Delta l = \frac{FL}{AY}\). Set equal: \(r_{cu}^2 = \frac{Y_{cu}}{Y_{Al}} r_{Al}^2\). Solving gives diameter 2.3 mm.
name: Questions Item 6

text: The energy stored in a strained wire is given by
explanation: Energy stored = work done by load = average force × extension = \(\frac{F}{2} \times \Delta l = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{load} \times \text{extension}\).
name: Questions Item 7

text: A material has Poisson's ratio 0.50. If a uniform rod made of this material suffers a longitudinal strain of \(2 \times 10^{-3}\), then the percentage change in volume is
explanation: Volumetric strain = (1 - 2σ) × longitudinal strain = (1 - 2 × 0.5) × 2 × 10^{-3} = 0. Hence, percentage change = 0.
name: Questions Item 8

text: Young's modulus for perfectly rigid body is
explanation: For perfectly rigid body, strain = 0 for any stress. Young's modulus \(Y = \frac{\text{stress}}{\text{strain}} = \infty\).
name: Questions Item 9

text: According to the Hooke's law the force required to change the length of a wire by l is proportional to
explanation: From \(F = \frac{Y A}{l} \Delta l\), F ∝ Δl. Hence, force is proportional to extension l.
name: Questions Item 10

text: A wire of length 1 m and radius 2 mm is vertically clamped. The lower end is twisted through an angle of 45°. The angle of shear is
explanation: Angle of shear \(\phi = \frac{r \theta}{l} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 45^\circ}{1} = 0.09^\circ\).
name: Questions Item 11

text: What is the pressure required to reduce the given volume of water by 1%? (Bulk modulus (K) = 2 × 10^8 Nm^{-2})
explanation: Bulk modulus \(B = \frac{p}{\Delta V / V}\). \(\Delta V / V = 0.01\). Hence, \(p = 2 \times 10^8 \times 0.01 = 2 \times 10^6\) Nm^{-2}.
name: Questions Item 12

text: A solid copper cube of 7 cm edge is subjected to a hydraulic pressure of 8000 kPa. The volume contraction of the copper cube is (Bulk modulus of copper = 140 GPa)
explanation: \(B = \frac{pV}{\Delta V}\). \(V = (7)^3 = 343\) cm³. \(\Delta V = \frac{8000 \times 10^3 \times 343}{140 \times 10^9} = 19.6 \times 10^{-3}\) cm³.
name: Questions Item 13

text: A one metre steel wire of negligible mass and area of cross-section 0.01 cm² is kept on a smooth horizontal table with one end fixed. A ball of mass 1 kg is attached to the other end. The ball and the wire are rotating with an angular velocity of ω. If the elongation of the wire is 2 mm, then ω is (Young's modulus of steel = 2 × 10^{11} Nm^{-2})
explanation: \(Y = \frac{T l}{A \Delta l}\). Tension T = m ω² l. Solving gives ω = 20 rad s^{-1}.
name: Questions Item 14

text: The following four wires are made of the same material. If same tension is applied to each, the wire having largest extension is
explanation: Extension \(\Delta l \propto \frac{l}{d^2}\). For first wire, \(\frac{0.5}{(0.5)^2} = 2\) (maximum).
name: Questions Item 15

text: Two metal plates P and Q of same material are arranged as shown in the figure. If both the plates are uniformly heated through the same range of temperature, then
explanation: Heating causes uniform expansion in all directions. Both gaps x and y decrease.
name: Questions Item 16

text: Two wires of equal length and equal cross sectional areas are suspended as shown in the figure. Their Young’s moduli are Y₁ and Y₂, respectively. The equivalent Young’s modulus is
explanation: Effective Y for parallel combination: \(Y = \frac{Y_1 + Y_2}{2}\).
name: Questions Item 17

text: The area of cross-section of steel wire is 0.1 cm² and Young’s modulus of steel is 2 × 10^{11} N m^{-2}. The force required to stretched by 0.1% of its length is
name: Questions Item 18

text: A uniform wire of length 10 m and diameter 0.6 mm is stretched by 6 mm with certain force. If the Poisson’s ratio of the material of the wire is 0.3, then the change in diameter of the wire is
name: Questions Item 19

text: Two equal and opposite forces each F act on a rod of uniform cross-sectional area a as shown in the figure. Shearing stress on the section AB will be
explanation: Shear stress = \(\frac{F \sin \theta}{a} \cos \theta\).
name: Questions Item 20

text: A sphere of mass 2 kg and diameter 4.5 cm is attached to the lower end of a steel wire of 2 m length and area of cross-section 0.24 × 10^{-6} m². The wire is suspended from 205 cm high ceiling of a room. When the system is made to oscillate as a simple pendulum, the sphere just grazes the floor at its lowest position. The velocity of the sphere at the lowest position is (Young's modulus of steel = 2 × 10^{11} Nm^{-2})
explanation: Extension Δl = 0.5 cm. Using Y = (Mg + M v²/R) L / (A Δl) gives v = 10 ms^{-1}.
name: Questions Item 21

text: A rubber cube of side 5 cm has one face fixed, while a tangential force 1800 N is applied on its opposite face. If modulus of rigidity of rubber is 2.4 × 10^6 Nm^{-2}, then the lateral displacement of the strained face is
explanation: Shear strain = F/(A η) = 1800 / (0.05² × 2.4×10^6) = 0.3. Lateral displacement = strain × side = 0.3 × 5 cm = 15 mm.
name: Questions Item 22
